subject: Physics
question_text: Given the mass of iron nucleus as 55.85u and A = 56, find the nuclear density?
question_html: Given the mass of iron nucleus as 55.85u and A = 56, find the nuclear density?
solution_text: m Fe = 55.85u = 9.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –26 kg Nuclear density = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{9.27 \times 10^{-26}}{(4 \pi / 3)\left(1.2 \times 10^{-15}\right)^{3}} \times \frac{1}{56}{/tex} = 2.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 17 kg m –3 The density of matter in neutron stars (an astrophysical object) is comparable to this density. This shows that matter in these objects has been compressed to such an extent that they resemble a big nucleus.
solution_html:
mFe = 55.85u = 9.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–26 kg
Nuclear density = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{9.27 \times 10^{-26}}{(4 \pi / 3)\left(1.2 \times 10^{-15}\right)^{3}} \times \frac{1}{56}{/tex} = 2.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 kg m–3
The density of matter in neutron stars (an astrophysical object) is comparable to this density. This shows that matter in these objects has been compressed to such an extent that they resemble a big nucleus.

subject: Physics
question_text: Calculate the energy equivalent of 1 g of substance.
question_html: Calculate the energy equivalent of 1 g of substance.
solution_text: Energy, E = 10 –3 {tex}\times{/tex} ( 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ) 2 J E = 10 –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 16 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 13 J Thus, if one gram of matter is converted to energy, there is a release of enormous amount of energy.
solution_html:
Energy, E = 10–3 {tex}\times{/tex} ( 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)2 J
E = 10–3 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013 J
Thus, if one gram of matter is converted to energy, there is a release of enormous amount of energy.

subject: Physics
question_text: Find the energy equivalent of one atomic mass unit, first in Joules and then in MeV. Using this, express the mass defect of {tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex} in MeV/c 2 .
question_html: Find the energy equivalent of one atomic mass unit, first in Joules and then in MeV. Using this, express the mass defect of {tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex} in MeV/c2.
solution_text: 1amu = 1.6605 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 kg To convert it into energy units, we multiply it by c 2 and find that energy equivalent = 1.6605 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 {tex}\times{/tex} (2.9979 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ) 2 kg m 2 /s = 1.4924 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –10 J {tex}=\frac{1.4924 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 0.9315 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 eV = 931.5 MeV or, 1amu = 931.5 MeV/c 2 For 16 {tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex} , {tex}\triangle{/tex} M = 0.13691 u = 0.13691 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 MeV/c 2 = 127.5 MeV/c 2 this mass defect appears in the form of energy needed to separate {tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex} into its constituents is thus 127.5 MeV/c 2 .
solution_html:
1amu = 1.6605 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–27 kg
To convert it into energy units, we multiply it by c2 and find that energy equivalent = 1.6605 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–27 {tex}\times{/tex} (2.9979 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)2 kg m2/s
= 1.4924 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 J
{tex}=\frac{1.4924 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
= 0.9315 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 eV
= 931.5 MeV
or, 1amu = 931.5 MeV/c2
For 16
{tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex}, {tex}\triangle{/tex}M = 0.13691 u = 0.13691 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 MeV/c2
= 127.5 MeV/c2
this mass defect appears in the  form of energy needed to separate {tex}{}_{8}^{16}O{/tex} into its constituents is thus 127.5 MeV/c2.

subject: Physics
question_text: Answer the following questions: Are the equations of nuclear reactions ‘balanced’ in the sense a chemical equation (e.g., 2H 2 + O 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H 2 O) is? If not, in what sense are they balanced on both sides? If both the number of protons and the number of neutrons are conserved in each nuclear reaction, in what way is mass converted into energy (or vice-versa) in a nuclear reaction? A general impression exists that mass-energy interconversion takes place only in nuclear reaction and never in chemical reaction. This is strictly speaking, incorrect. Explain.
question_html:
Answer the following questions:

  1. Are the equations of nuclear reactions ‘balanced’ in the sense a chemical equation (e.g., 2H2 + O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O) is? If not, in what sense are they balanced on both sides?
  2. If both the number of protons and the number of neutrons are conserved in each nuclear reaction, in what way is mass converted into energy (or vice-versa) in a nuclear reaction?
  3. A general impression exists that mass-energy interconversion takes place only in nuclear reaction and never in chemical reaction. This is strictly speaking, incorrect. Explain.
solution_text: A chemical reaction simply changes the original combination of atoms. A chemical equation is balanced in the sense that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation. A chemical reaction merely alters the original combinations of atoms. In a nuclear reaction, elements may be transmuted. Thus, the number of atoms of each element is not necessarily conserved in a nuclear reaction. However, the number of protons and the number of neutrons are both separately conserved in a nuclear reaction. In nuclear reactions, the number of protons and the number of neutrons are the same on the two sides of the equation. We know that the binding energy of a nucleus gives a negative contribution to the mass of the nucleus . Now, since the proton number and neutron number are conserved in a nuclear reaction, the total rest mass of neutrons and protons is the same on either side of a reaction. But the total binding energy of nuclei on the left side need not be the same as that on the right-hand side. The difference in these binding energies appears as the energy released or absorbed in a nuclear reaction. Since binding energy contributes to mass, we say that the difference in the total mass of nuclei on the two sides gets converted into energy or vice-versa. It is in this sense that a nuclear reaction is an example of mass-energy interconversion. From the point of view of mass-energy interconversion, a chemical reaction is similar to a nuclear reaction in principle. The energy released or absorbed in a chemical reaction can be traced to the difference in chemical binding energies of atoms and molecules on the two sides of a reaction. Since, strictly speaking, chemical binding energy also gives a negative contribution (mass defect) to the total mass of an atom or molecule, we can equally well say that the difference in the total mass of atoms or molecules, on the two sides of the chemical reaction gets converted into energy or vice-versa. However, the mass defects involved in a chemical reaction are almost a million times smaller than those in a nuclear reaction. That is why we have the impression, that mass-energy interconversion does not take place in a chemical reaction , though the impression is incorrect.
solution_html:
1. A chemical reaction simply changes the original combination of atoms. A chemical equation is balanced in the sense that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation. A chemical reaction merely alters the original combinations of atoms. In a nuclear reaction, elements may be transmuted. Thus, the number of atoms of each element is not necessarily conserved in a nuclear reaction. However, the number of protons and the number of neutrons are both separately conserved in a nuclear reaction.  In nuclear reactions, the number of protons and the number of neutrons are the same on the two sides of the equation.
  2. We know that the binding energy of a nucleus gives a negative contribution to the mass of the nucleus . Now, since the proton number and neutron number are conserved in a nuclear reaction, the total rest mass of neutrons and protons is the same on either side of a reaction. But the total binding energy of nuclei on the left side need not be the same as that on the right-hand side. The difference in these binding energies appears as the energy released or absorbed in a nuclear reaction. Since binding energy contributes to mass, we say that the difference in the total mass of nuclei on the two sides gets converted into energy or vice-versa. It is in this sense that a nuclear reaction is an example of mass-energy interconversion.
  3. From the point of view of mass-energy interconversion, a chemical reaction is similar to a nuclear reaction in principle. The energy released or absorbed in a chemical reaction can be traced to the difference in chemical binding energies of atoms and molecules on the two sides of a reaction. Since, strictly speaking, chemical binding energy also gives a negative contribution (mass defect) to the total mass of an atom or molecule, we can equally well say that the difference in the total mass of atoms or molecules, on the two sides of the chemical reaction gets converted into energy or vice-versa. However, the mass defects involved in a chemical reaction are almost a million times smaller than those in a nuclear reaction. That is why we have the  impression, that mass-energy interconversion does not take place in a chemical reaction , though the impression is incorrect.

subject: Physics
question_text: Obtain the binding energy (in MeV) of a nitrogen nucleus {tex}(^{14}_7 N){/tex} , given {tex}m({}_7^{14}N){/tex} = 14.00307 u
question_html: Obtain the binding energy (in MeV) of a nitrogen nucleus {tex}(^{14}_7 N){/tex}, given {tex}m({}_7^{14}N){/tex} = 14.00307 u
solution_text: Atomic mass of nitrogen {tex}\left(_{7} N^{14}\right){/tex} , m = 14.00307 u A nucleus of nitrogen {tex}_{7} N^{14}{/tex} contain s 7 protons and 7 neutrons. Hence, the mass defect of this nucleus, {tex}\Delta m=7 m_{H}+7 m_{n}-m{/tex} Where, Mass of a proton, m H = 1.007825 u Mass of a neutron, m n = 1.008665 u {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} m = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665 - 14.00307 = 7.054775 + 7.06055 - 14.00307 = 0.11236 u But 1u = 931.5 MeV/c 2 {tex}\therefore \Delta m=0.11236 \times 931.5 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^{2}{/tex} Hence, the binding energy of the nucleus is given as: {tex}E_b=\Delta m c^{2}{/tex} Where, c = Speed of light {tex}\therefore{/tex} E b = 0.11236 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 {tex}\left(\frac{M e V}{c^{2}}\right) \times c^{2}{/tex} = 104.66334 MeV Hence, the binding energy of a nitrogen nucleus is 104.66334 MeV.
solution_html:
Atomic mass of nitrogen{tex}\left(_{7} N^{14}\right){/tex}, m = 14.00307 u
A nucleus of nitrogen {tex}_{7} N^{14}{/tex} contain s 7 protons and 7 neutrons.
Hence, the mass defect of this nucleus,  {tex}\Delta m=7 m_{H}+7 m_{n}-m{/tex} 
Where,
Mass of a proton, mH = 1.007825 u
Mass of a neutron, mn = 1.008665 u
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}m = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665 - 14.00307
= 7.054775 + 7.06055 - 14.00307
= 0.11236 u
But 1u = 931.5 MeV/c2 
{tex}\therefore \Delta m=0.11236 \times 931.5 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^{2}{/tex} 
Hence, the binding energy of the nucleus is given as:
{tex}E_b=\Delta m c^{2}{/tex}
Where,
c =  Speed of light
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Eb = 0.11236 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5  {tex}\left(\frac{M e V}{c^{2}}\right) \times c^{2}{/tex} 
= 104.66334 MeV
Hence, the binding energy of a nitrogen nucleus is 104.66334 MeV.

subject: Physics
question_text: Obtain the binding energy of the nuclei {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} and {tex}{}_{83}^{209}Bi{/tex} in units of MeV from the following data: m ( {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} ) = 55.934939 u m ( {tex}{}_{83}^{209}Bi{/tex} ) = 208.980388 u
question_html:
Obtain the binding energy of the nuclei {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} and {tex}{}_{83}^{209}Bi{/tex} in units of MeV from the following data:
m ( {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex}) = 55.934939 u
m ( {tex}{}_{83}^{209}Bi{/tex} ) = 208.980388 u
solution_text: The number of neutron and Proton both in a 56 Fe 26 atom is respectively 30 and 26. The mass defect {tex}\triangle{/tex} m = m p + m n - m Fe And Binding energy is given by E B = {tex}\triangle{/tex} mc 2 Mass of a proton = 1.007825 u Mass of a neutron = 1.008665 u In this case mass defect = Δm = (26 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665-55.934939)u = 0.528461u We know, 1a.m. u = 931.5 MeV/c 2 So, E = {tex}\triangle{/tex} mc 2 = 0.528461, {tex}\triangle{/tex} 931.5(MeV/c 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = 492.26MeV So, Binding Energy per Nucleon = {tex}\frac{\text { Total Binding Energy }}{\text { Total No Of Nucleon }}=\frac{492.26}{56}{/tex} = 8.79MeV The number of neutron and Proton both in a 209 Bi 83 atom is respectively 126 and 83. The mass defect Δm = m p + m n - m Bi And Binding energy is given by E B = {tex}\triangle{/tex} mc 2 Mass of a proton = 1.007825 u Mass of a neutron = 1.008665 u Mass Defect = {tex}\triangle{/tex} m = 83 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 126 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665 - 208.980388 = 1.760877 u We know, 1a.m.u = 931.5 MeV/c 2 So, E = {tex}\triangle{/tex} mc 2 = 1.760877 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 (MeV/c 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = 1640.26 MeV Average binding energy per nucleon = {tex}\frac {1640.26}{209}{/tex} = 7.848 MeV Hence, these are the required values.
solution_html:
The number of neutron and Proton both in a 56Fe26 atom is respectively 30 and 26.
The mass defect {tex}\triangle{/tex}m = mp + mn - mFe
And Binding energy is given by EB = {tex}\triangle{/tex}mc2
Mass of a proton = 1.007825 u
Mass of a neutron = 1.008665 u
In this case mass defect = Δm = (26 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665-55.934939)u = 0.528461u
We know, 1a.m. u = 931.5 MeV/c2
So, E = {tex}\triangle{/tex}mc2 = 0.528461, {tex}\triangle{/tex} 931.5(MeV/c2) {tex}\times{/tex} c2 = 492.26MeV
So, Binding Energy per Nucleon = {tex}\frac{\text { Total Binding Energy }}{\text { Total No Of Nucleon }}=\frac{492.26}{56}{/tex} = 8.79MeV
The number of neutron and Proton both in a 209Bi83 atom is respectively 126 and 83.
The mass defect Δm = mp + mn - mBi
And Binding energy is given by EB = {tex}\triangle{/tex}mc2
Mass of a proton = 1.007825 u
Mass of a neutron = 1.008665 u
Mass Defect = {tex}\triangle{/tex}m = 83 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.007825 + 126 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008665 - 208.980388 = 1.760877 u
We know, 1a.m.u = 931.5 MeV/c2
So, E = {tex}\triangle{/tex}mc2 = 1.760877 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 (MeV/c2) {tex}\times{/tex} c2 = 1640.26 MeV
Average binding energy per nucleon = {tex}\frac {1640.26}{209}{/tex} = 7.848 MeV

Hence, these are the required values.

subject: Physics
question_text: A given coin has a mass of 3.0 g. Calculate the nuclear energy that would be required to separate all the neutrons and protons from each other. For simplicity assume that the coin is entirely made of {tex}_{29}^{63}Cu{/tex} atoms ( of mass 62.92960 u)
question_html: A given coin has a mass of 3.0 g. Calculate the nuclear energy that would be required to separate all the neutrons and protons from each other. For simplicity assume that the coin is entirely made of {tex}_{29}^{63}Cu{/tex} atoms ( of mass 62.92960 u)
solution_text: Mass of atom = 62.92960 u Mass of 29 electrons {tex} = 29 \times 0.000548 u{/tex} = 0.015892 u Mass of nucleus = (62.9296 - 0.015892)u = 62.913708 u Mass of 29 protons {tex} = 29 \times 1.007825\;u{/tex} = 29.226925 u Mass of (63 - 29) i.e. 34 neutrons {tex} = 34 \times 1.008665\;u{/tex} = 34.29461 u Total mass of protons and neutrons = (29.226925 + 34.29461) u = 63.521535 u Binding energy = {tex}\left( {63.521535 - 62.913708} \right) \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex} {tex} = 0.607827 \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex} Number of atoms in 3g coin {tex}=\frac{6.023\times10^{23}\times3}{63}=2.868\times10^{22}{/tex} Therefore, Required energy {tex} = 2.868\times10^{22} \times 0.607827 \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex} {tex} = 1.6 \times {10^{25}}MeV = 2.6 \times {10^{12}}J{/tex}
solution_html:
Mass of atom = 62.92960 u
Mass of 29 electrons {tex} = 29 \times 0.000548 u{/tex}
= 0.015892 u
Mass of nucleus = (62.9296 - 0.015892)u
= 62.913708 u
Mass of 29 protons {tex} = 29 \times 1.007825\;u{/tex}
= 29.226925 u
Mass of (63 - 29) i.e. 34 neutrons
{tex} = 34 \times 1.008665\;u{/tex}
= 34.29461 u
Total mass of protons and neutrons
= (29.226925 + 34.29461) u
= 63.521535 u
Binding energy = {tex}\left( {63.521535 - 62.913708} \right) \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex}
{tex} = 0.607827 \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex}
Number of atoms in 3g coin {tex}=\frac{6.023\times10^{23}\times3}{63}=2.868\times10^{22}{/tex}
Therefore, Required energy {tex} = 2.868\times10^{22} \times 0.607827 \times 931.5\;MeV{/tex}
{tex} = 1.6 \times {10^{25}}MeV = 2.6 \times {10^{12}}J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Obtain approximately the ratio of the nuclear radii of the gold isotope {tex}_{79}^{197}Au{/tex} and the silver isotope {tex}_{47}^{107}Ag{/tex} .
question_html: Obtain approximately the ratio of the nuclear radii of the gold isotope {tex}_{79}^{197}Au{/tex} and the silver isotope {tex}_{47}^{107}Ag{/tex}.
solution_html:
As, {tex}R \approx {A^{1/3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex}
{tex} = {\left( {\frac{{197}}{{107}}} \right)^{1/3}} = {(1.84)^{1/3}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\log _{10}}\left( {\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = {\log _{10}}{(1.84)^{1/3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\log _{10}}\left( {\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{3}{\log _{10}}(1.84){/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.2648{/tex} = 0.08827
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}}{/tex} = antilog (0.08827) = 1.23

subject: Physics
question_text: The Q value of a nuclear reaction A + b {tex}\to{/tex} C + d is defined by Q = [m A + m b – m C – m d ]c 2 where the masses refer to the respective nuclei. Determine from the given data the Q-value of the following reactions and state whether the reactions are exothermic or endothermic. {tex}_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex} {tex}_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C}+_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}+_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex} Atomic masses are given to be m ( {tex}_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex} ) = 2.014102 u m ( {tex}_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H}{/tex} ) = 3.016049 u m ( {tex}{}_{6}^{12}C{/tex} ) = 12.000000 u m ( {tex}_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}{/tex} ) = 19.992439 u
question_html:
The Q value of a nuclear reaction A + b {tex}\to{/tex} C + d is defined by Q = [mA + mb – mC – md]c2 where the masses refer to the respective nuclei. Determine from the given data the Q-value of the following reactions and state whether the reactions are exothermic or endothermic.

  1. {tex}_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex}
  2. {tex}_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C}+_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}+_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex}

Atomic masses are given to be
m ({tex}_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex}) = 2.014102 u
m ({tex}_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H}{/tex}) = 3.016049 u
m ({tex}{}_{6}^{12}C{/tex}) = 12.000000 u
m ({tex}_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}{/tex}) = 19.992439 u
solution_text: The given equation is: {tex}_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex} Hence, according to given data and given definition of Q value in the question Q value = [m( 1 H 1 ) + m( 3 H 1 ) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} m( 2 H 1 )] {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = (-0.00433u {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 ) = -0.00433 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 MeV = -4.0334 MeV The negative value of Q value suggests this reaction is endothermic. The reaction is : {tex}^{12} \mathrm{C}+_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}+_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex} According to the question, the Q-value of the reaction can be written as :- Q = [2 {tex}\times{/tex} m( 12 C 6 )-m( 20 Ne 10 )-m( 4 He 2 )] {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = (0.004958u) {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = 0.004958 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 = 4.6183MeV Here, the Q value is positive hence reaction is exothermic.
solution_html:
1. The given equation is: {tex}_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{3} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}+_{1}^{2} \mathrm{H}{/tex}
    Hence, according to given data and given definition of Q value in the question
    Q value = [m(1H1) + m(3H1) -  2 {tex}\times{/tex} m(2H1)] {tex}\times{/tex} c2
    = (-0.00433u {tex}\times{/tex} c2) = -0.00433 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 MeV
    = -4.0334 MeV
    The negative value of Q value suggests this reaction is endothermic.
  2. The reaction is : {tex}^{12} \mathrm{C}+_{6}^{12} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}+_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex}
    According to the question, the Q-value of the reaction can be written as :-
    Q = [2 {tex}\times{/tex} m(12C6)-m(20Ne10)-m(4He2)] {tex}\times{/tex} c2
    = (0.004958u) {tex}\times{/tex} c2
    = 0.004958 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5
    = 4.6183MeV
    Here, the Q value is positive hence reaction is exothermic.

subject: Physics
question_text: Suppose, we think of fission of a {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} nucleus into two equal fragments, {tex}{}_{13}^{28}Al{/tex} . Is the fission energetically possible? Argue by working out Q of the process. Given m ( {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} ) = 55.93494 u and m ( {tex}{}_{13}^{28}Al{/tex} ) = 27.98191 u.
question_html: Suppose, we think of fission of a {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex} nucleus into two equal fragments, {tex}{}_{13}^{28}Al{/tex}. Is the fission energetically possible? Argue by working out Q of the process. Given m ({tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe{/tex}) = 55.93494 u and m ({tex}{}_{13}^{28}Al{/tex}) = 27.98191 u.
solution_text: The fission reaction can be given as: {tex}_{26}^{56} \mathrm{Fe} \rightarrow 2_{13}^{28} \mathrm{Al}{/tex} The Q value for this reaction will be given as = [m( {tex}_{26}^{56} \mathrm{Fe}{/tex} ) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} m( {tex}2_{13}^{28} \mathrm{Al}{/tex} )] {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = (55.93494 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 27.98191)u {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = -0.02888u {tex}\times{/tex} c 2 = -0.02888 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 (1 u = 931.5 MeV/c 2 ) = -26.902 MeV The Q value is negative which suggests this reaction is endothermic, but we know fission reactions are exothermic. Hence, this fission reaction is not energetically possible.
solution_html:
The fission reaction can be given as: {tex}_{26}^{56} \mathrm{Fe} \rightarrow 2_{13}^{28} \mathrm{Al}{/tex}
The Q value for this reaction will be given as = [m({tex}_{26}^{56} \mathrm{Fe}{/tex}) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} m({tex}2_{13}^{28} \mathrm{Al}{/tex})] {tex}\times{/tex} c2
= (55.93494 -  2 {tex}\times{/tex} 27.98191)u {tex}\times{/tex} c2 
= -0.02888u {tex}\times{/tex} c2 
= -0.02888 {tex}\times{/tex} 931.5 (1 u = 931.5 MeV/c2)
= -26.902 MeV
The Q value is negative which suggests this reaction is endothermic, but we know fission reactions are exothermic. Hence, this fission reaction is not energetically possible.
